content.text: Log probabilities (
logprobs

) expose how confident an LLM is in each token it generates — the natural log of the probability assigned to the chosen token at that position. APIs can return logprobs for every output token, along with the top-k alternative tokens the model considered. In agentic systems, logprobs power confidence gating, plan reranking, routing decisions, and debugging, all without extra model calls.
What it is

At each decoding step the model produces a vector of raw scores (logits) over the entire vocabulary. The softmax operation converts these into a probability distribution summing to 1. The logprob for chosen token w at position t is log P(w | context), where P is the softmax probability. Because probabilities lie in (0, 1], their logs lie in (-inf, 0]. A logprob of 0 would mean P = 1 (absolute certainty); -0.01 means very high confidence; -3.0 is moderate uncertainty; values below -5 indicate the model barely chose that token over many alternatives.

When you set logprobs=True and top_logprobs=k in an OpenAI-compatible API call, each token in the response carries its logprob plus up to k alternative tokens and their logprobs. This lets you reconstruct the local probability distribution at every position.
Why it matters

Without logprobs, you see only the model's text output — a binary success-or-failure view. Binary feedback hides near-misses: the model may have chosen the correct tool name with probability 0.55, meaning a different random seed would have chosen the wrong tool with probability 0.45. Logprobs surface this uncertainty without requiring you to run the model again with a different prompt or sampling temperature.

In agentic pipelines where an incorrect tool call may trigger real-world side effects (a database write, an API call, a financial transaction), uncertainty awareness is critical. Logprobs provide a lightweight, in-band uncertainty signal that can gate irreversible actions or route them to human review. They also power prompt regression testing: if a prompt change causes certain token logprobs to drop sharply, you can catch the regression before users see it.
Benefits
Exposes per-token model uncertainty without extra model inference passes
Enables reranking of multiple sampled agent plans by mean or minimum logprob
Flags ambiguous tool-name or parameter choices before execution
Supports prompt regression detection by tracking logprob distributions across prompt versions
Allows cheap confidence gating — escalate low-confidence completions to human review
Provides interpretable debugging signal when model behaviour changes unexpectedly
Compatible with streaming responses on most providers, enabling early-exit on low-confidence tokens
Trade-offs
High logprob does not imply factual correctness — confident hallucinations exist and are common
Logprob calibration varies significantly between model families and even fine-tunes
Averaging over all tokens conflates high-confidence boilerplate with low-confidence payload tokens
Minimum logprob is sensitive to punctuation, whitespace, and single unusual tokens
Extra top_logprobs data increases response payload size and latency marginally
API field names and streaming support differ across providers (OpenAI, Azure, Anthropic, Cohere)
Temperature of 0 concentrates the distribution, making logprobs appear artificially high
How it works

The model runs a forward pass through the transformer, ending with a linear projection of the final hidden state onto the vocabulary. This produces a vector z of dimension V (vocabulary size, typically 50,000-200,000). Softmax normalises z into probabilities: P(w) = exp(z_w) / sum_i exp(z_i). The API then computes log P(w) for the sampled token and for the top-k tokens by descending probability. These values are returned alongside the token text.

Aggregate scores are computed by collecting all per-token logprobs over a completion span and reducing them. Common reductions are: mean logprob (typical quality of each token), min logprob (worst single-token confidence in the span), and length-normalised log-probability sum (equivalent to mean logprob). The length-normalised form is preferred for comparing completions of different lengths because longer completions accumulate more negative values even if their per-token confidence is equal.

Mental model: think of logprobs as a confidence thermometer at each decoding step. A thermometer near 0°C means the model is barely above freezing on that choice; near 100°C means it was essentially certain. The thermometer reads low temperature even when the text reads fluently — fluency and probability are related but not identical.
Architecture

The diagram traces the full logprobs pipeline. An input context passes through the LLM's forward pass, producing raw logits. Softmax converts these to probabilities; log() yields logprobs at or below zero. The API response bundles the chosen token's logprob plus the top-k alternatives. The Agent Confidence Gate evaluates whether mean logprob clears the threshold: confident completions proceed to execution; uncertain completions route to the Reranker / Router, which picks the best of N sampled plans or escalates to human review.
When to use it
Before executing irreversible agentic actions (writes, sends, purchases) based on model decisions
When comparing N sampled completions and you need to pick the best one cheaply
When debugging a prompt regression — logprob drops pinpoint which token the model became less sure of
For routing: low-confidence answers go to a stronger model or human; high-confidence ones stay on the cheap model
When building analytics dashboards for prompt engineers to track distribution sharpness over time
When generating classification labels and you want the probability assigned to each class token

Avoid using logprobs as the sole gate when latency is extremely tight and the marginal cost of human review is higher than the cost of occasional errors. Also avoid them as a substitute for schema validation — a syntactically invalid JSON can carry high logprobs if the model has learned to output it confidently but incorrectly.
How to implement
Basic logprob retrieval

The minimal change to a standard chat completion call is adding logprobs=True. Optionally add top_logprobs=k (1–20 on OpenAI) to get ranked alternatives. The response structure gains a logprobs field on each choice, containing a content array of ChatCompletionTokenLogprob objects.

from openai import OpenAI
import math

client = OpenAI()

def get_completion_with_logprobs(prompt: str, top_k: int = 5) -> dict:
    """
    Request a chat completion and return tokens with their logprobs.
    top_k: how many alternative tokens to expose at each position.
    """
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        logprobs=True,
        top_logprobs=top_k,
        temperature=0,
        max_tokens=200,
    )
    choice = resp.choices[0]
    tokens = []
    for lp in choice.logprobs.content:
        token_info = {
            "token": lp.token,
            "logprob": lp.logprob,
            "prob": math.exp(lp.logprob),        # convert to probability
            "alternatives": [
                {"token": alt.token, "logprob": alt.logprob, "prob": math.exp(alt.logprob)}
                for alt in lp.top_logprobs
            ],
        }
        tokens.append(token_info)
    return {"text": choice.message.content, "tokens": tokens}


# Example: inspect token-level confidence on a structured output
result = get_completion_with_logprobs(
    'Return only JSON: {"intent": "book_flight", "destination": "Paris"}'
)
print("Full response:", result["text"])
print()
for t in result["tokens"]:
    marker = "  LOW" if t["logprob"] < -1.5 else ""
    print(f"  {t['token']!r:20s} lp={t['logprob']:.3f}  p={t['prob']:.3f}{marker}")
    if t["logprob"] < -1.5:
        for alt in t["alternatives"][:3]:
            print(f"       alt {alt['token']!r:15s} lp={alt['logprob']:.3f}")
Confidence gating with N samples

Generate multiple completions at non-zero temperature, score each by mean logprob, and apply a threshold. The threshold is a hyperparameter — tune it on a labelled set of agent successes and failures for your specific task.

import math
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def score_completion(messages: list[dict], temperature: float = 0.7) -> tuple[str, float]:
    """
    Generate one completion and return (text, mean_logprob_over_completion_tokens).
    Mean logprob is the length-normalised log-probability of the response.
    """
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=messages,
        logprobs=True,
        temperature=temperature,
        max_tokens=300,
    )
    choice = resp.choices[0]
    lps = [lp.logprob for lp in choice.logprobs.content]
    mean_lp = sum(lps) / len(lps) if lps else -999.0
    return choice.message.content, mean_lp


def confidence_gate(
    messages: list[dict],
    threshold: float = -1.2,
    n_samples: int = 3,
    temperature: float = 0.7,
) -> dict:
    """
    Generate N completions, score each by mean logprob, and apply a confidence gate.
    Returns the best completion if its score is above threshold; otherwise escalates.

    threshold: log-probability cutoff (tune on your task with labelled failures).
    n_samples: number of independent samples to consider.
    """
    samples = [score_completion(messages, temperature) for _ in range(n_samples)]
    best_text, best_score = max(samples, key=lambda x: x[1])

    if best_score >= threshold:
        return {"status": "confident", "text": best_text, "score": best_score}
    else:
        return {
            "status": "low_confidence",
            "text": best_text,
            "score": best_score,
            "action": "escalate_to_human_or_clarify",
        }


# Usage: agent tool-call decision
messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a travel booking agent. Answer with JSON only."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Book a flight to Lon from JFK next Tuesday."},
]
result = confidence_gate(messages, threshold=-1.2, n_samples=3)
print(f"Status : {result['status']}")
print(f"Score  : {result['score']:.3f}")
print(f"Text   : {result['text']}")
if result["status"] == "low_confidence":
    print("Action :", result["action"])
Plan reranking

Reranking is a cheap quality filter: instead of running a separate evaluator model, use the generative model's own confidence as a proxy ranking signal. Generate N plans, sort by (mean_logprob, min_logprob) descending, and execute the top-ranked plan.

import math
from openai import OpenAI
from dataclasses import dataclass

client = OpenAI()

@dataclass
class RankedPlan:
    text: str
    mean_logprob: float
    min_logprob: float
    token_count: int

def rerank_plans(system_prompt: str, user_query: str, n: int = 5) -> list[RankedPlan]:
    """
    Generate N agent plans, score each by (mean logprob, min logprob), and sort descending.
    min_logprob flags completions that contain at least one very low-confidence token —
    important for catching tool-name or parameter guesses buried in otherwise fluent text.
    """
    plans = []
    for _ in range(n):
        resp = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o-mini",
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
                {"role": "user",   "content": user_query},
            ],
            logprobs=True,
            temperature=0.9,
            max_tokens=400,
        )
        choice = resp.choices[0]
        lps = [lp.logprob for lp in choice.logprobs.content]
        if not lps:
            continue
        plans.append(RankedPlan(
            text=choice.message.content,
            mean_logprob=sum(lps) / len(lps),
            min_logprob=min(lps),
            token_count=len(lps),
        ))

    # primary sort by mean logprob descending; tie-break by min logprob
    plans.sort(key=lambda p: (p.mean_logprob, p.min_logprob), reverse=True)
    return plans


# Example
sys_prompt = "You are a data-analysis agent. Produce a step-by-step plan in JSON."
query = "Analyse monthly sales data and highlight anomalies."
ranked = rerank_plans(sys_prompt, query, n=5)
for i, plan in enumerate(ranked):
    print(f"Rank {i+1}  mean_lp={plan.mean_logprob:.3f}  min_lp={plan.min_logprob:.3f}  tokens={plan.token_count}")
print()
print("Best plan:")
print(ranked[0].text)
Segmented scoring for tool calls

Averaging logprobs over the full completion conflates low-entropy structural tokens (braces, quotes) with high-entropy payload tokens (tool names, parameter values). A more accurate signal segments the completion and scores only the semantically important span.
Examples
Example 1: classification confidence

When asking the model to classify sentiment as "positive", "negative", or "neutral", the logprob of the chosen label token directly gives you the model's probability for that class. If logprob("positive") = -0.1 you have P ≈ 0.9 confidence; if logprob("positive") = -1.6 you have P ≈ 0.2, meaning the model was nearly as likely to output a different label. This is a calibrated probability estimate — often more reliable than asking the model to output a confidence score in plain text.
Example 2: near-tie detection

When the top alternative token has a logprob within 0.5 of the chosen token, the model was nearly indifferent between two options. For tool selection this is dangerous: if logprob("search_database") = -0.4 and logprob("search_web") = -0.8, the model was 3x more likely to call search_database but search_web was still plausible. Surfacing the near-tie lets you present both options for human disambiguation instead of silently picking one.
Example 3: prompt regression testing

After a prompt change, compare the mean logprob of the tool-call span across a held-out evaluation set. If mean logprob drops from -0.35 to -0.85, the new prompt made the model significantly less decisive on tool selection, even if raw accuracy on the eval set is unchanged. Catching this shift early prevents silent degradation in production.
Real-world example

A travel booking agent is given: "Book me a flight from JFK to Lon next Tuesday." The ambiguity is Lon — London (LHR/LGW/LCY) or Long Beach (LGB). The agent calls a flight search tool. With logprobs enabled, the orchestrator inspects the logprob on the destination airport code token. If the model chose LHR with logprob = -0.2 and LGB was the top alternative at -2.1, the model was confident — proceed. But if LHR logprob = -0.7 and LGB = -0.9, the model was nearly tied — the orchestrator inserts a clarification step: "Did you mean London Heathrow (LHR) or Long Beach (LGB)?" This prevents a 00 flight to the wrong airport while adding only one turn of latency when needed and zero latency when the model is confident.
Common mistakes
Treating logprob as truth probability: a model can be confidently wrong on factual claims
Using a single global threshold copied from a paper without calibrating on domain-specific failure data
Averaging logprobs over prompt tokens as well as completion tokens — only completion tokens reflect the model's choices
Ignoring that temperature=0 artificially sharpens the distribution without making the model more accurate
Comparing logprobs across different models without recalibration — each model has its own probability scale
Using min logprob as the sole signal — a single punctuation token outlier can drag it down unfairly
Assuming logprob streaming is always available — some providers only support logprobs on non-streaming calls
Forgetting that top_logprobs consumes additional response tokens, increasing cost at high volume
Best practices
Score only completion tokens, never prompt tokens
Use mean logprob for ranking and min logprob as a secondary alert for suspect tokens
Calibrate thresholds on a labelled set of agent successes and failures for your specific task and model
Log the top-3 alternative tokens whenever chosen token logprob is below threshold — they reveal the confusion pair
Combine logprob gating with schema validation: logprobs catch uncertain content, schema validation catches malformed structure
Re-calibrate thresholds when switching models or fine-tunes — logprob scales shift between model families
Prefer length-normalised mean (mean logprob) over raw sum when comparing completions of unequal length
Document the threshold value and the calibration dataset version alongside your agent configuration
Performance & scalability

Enabling logprobs adds negligible latency on the model side because logprob computation reuses the softmax values already computed during generation. The cost is in response payload size: each token carries an additional float64 logprob value, and with top_logprobs=5, each token position carries 5 extra {token, logprob} pairs. At high throughput (millions of requests per day), this can meaningfully increase bandwidth costs.

For latency-critical paths, restrict top_logprobs to 1-3 instead of 20. If you only need to detect low-confidence completions without inspecting alternatives, set top_logprobs=0 (supported on some providers) to return only the chosen token's logprob. For reranking workloads, parallelise the N sample calls across async tasks — they are independent and can run concurrently.
top_logprobs=0 or 1 for lightweight confidence gating with minimal payload overhead
top_logprobs=5-10 for near-tie detection and debugging — run on a fraction of traffic, not all requests
Parallel async calls (asyncio.gather) for reranking N completions — wall time is max(individual latencies), not sum
Cache logprob metadata in your observability store, not in the critical path of the user response
Tooling & ecosystem
OpenAI Python SDK: logprobs=True, top_logprobs=k (1-20) on chat.completions.create
Azure OpenAI: same API surface as OpenAI SDK; logprob support tracks OpenAI capability releases
LiteLLM: normalises logprob response format across providers; check per-provider support via litellm.utils
LangChain: ChatOpenAI exposes logprobs via response_metadata after setting logprobs=True in model_kwargs
LlamaIndex: OpenAILLM passes logprobs through additional_kwargs on completion calls
Weights & Biases (Weave) / LangSmith: both can capture and visualise logprob traces across evaluation runs
Instructor: the library for structured outputs does not surface logprobs directly but is compatible when you access the raw OpenAI response via instructor.patch
Guidance / Outlines: constrained decoding libraries that manipulate logits directly — they expose similar per-token probability information but through a different interface

Only with local models

Logprobs are the cheapest signal: they add no extra inference cost and are available immediately. Their limitation is that they measure token-level confidence, not semantic correctness. Self-consistency voting measures whether N independent completions agree, which is a stronger correctness signal but costs N times the inference budget. A well-calibrated ensemble of logprob-based gating and self-consistency on critical decisions gives the best quality/cost ratio.
Security & reliability

Logprob thresholds are a defence-in-depth layer, not a primary security control. An adversarial prompt can cause the model to output malicious content confidently (high logprob), bypassing a logprob gate entirely. Never use logprob gating as the sole defence against prompt injection or content policy violations. Pair it with input validation, output schema enforcement, and content filters.
Do not expose raw logprobs to end users in production UI — they reveal model internals that could aid adversarial probing
Set threshold conservatively on high-stakes actions; prefer false positives (unnecessary human review) over false negatives
Audit threshold calibration regularly as the model is updated — provider model updates can shift logprob scales silently
Log calibration date and dataset alongside the threshold in version-controlled agent configuration
Consider separate thresholds for different action types: information retrieval can tolerate lower confidence than financial transactions
Key takeaways
Logprob = log P(chosen token | context); values are <= 0; closer to 0 means higher confidence
They expose per-token model uncertainty at generation time with no extra inference overhead
Primary uses: confidence gating before irreversible actions, plan reranking, prompt regression detection
Score only completion tokens — never mix prompt and completion logprobs in one average
High logprob does not imply factual accuracy; calibrate thresholds on labelled task-specific failure data
Pair with schema validation and content filters — logprobs alone are not a security or correctness guarantee
Re-calibrate thresholds whenever you switch models, fine-tunes, or significantly change your prompt
Mental model

Think of each decoding step as a weighted dice roll over the vocabulary. The logprob tells you how loaded the die was toward the chosen face. A logprob of -0.1 means the chosen face had about 90% of the weight; -2.3 means it had about 10%. The top_logprobs list shows you which other faces had significant weight. When several faces are nearly equal, the model is at a crossroads — that is when your confidence gate should intervene.
